subject: Physics
q: The two wires $A$ and $B$ of equal cross-section but of different materials are joined together. The ratio of Young's modulus of wire $A$ and wire $B$ is $20/11$. When the joined wire is kept under certain tension the elongations in the wires $A$ and $B$ are equal. If the length of wire $A$ is $2.2$ m, then the length of wire $B$ is _______ m.
question: The two wires $A$ and $B$ of equal cross-section but of different materials are joined together. The ratio of Young's modulus of wire $A$ and wire $B$ is $20/11$. When the joined wire is kept under certain tension the elongations in the wires $A$ and $B$ are equal. If the length of wire $A$ is $2.2$ m, then the length of wire $B$ is _______ m.
solution:
Given that the wires have equal cross-sectional area $A$ and are under the same tension $F$, the elongation $\Delta L$ is given by:

$\Delta L = \dfrac{F L}{A Y}$

Since the elongations in both wires are equal ($\Delta L_A = \Delta L_B$):

$\dfrac{F L_A}{A Y_A} = \dfrac{F L_B}{A Y_B}$

$\dfrac{L_A}{Y_A} = \dfrac{L_B}{Y_B}$

$L_B = L_A \times \dfrac{Y_B}{Y_A}$

Substituting the given values $L_A = 2.2$ m and $\dfrac{Y_A}{Y_B} = \dfrac{20}{11}$:

$L_B = 2.2 \times \dfrac{11}{20}$

$L_B = \dfrac{24.2}{20} = 1.21$ m

Answer: $1.21$

subject: Physics
q:
In an experiment, brass and steel wires of length 1 m each with areas of cross section 1 mm2 are used. The wires are connected in series and one end of the combined wire is connected to a rigid support and other end is subjected to elongation. The stress required to produce a net elongation of 0.2 mm is,
[Given, the Young's Modulus for steel and brass are, respectively, 120×109 N/m2 and 60×109 N/m2 ]
question:
In an experiment, brass and steel wires of length 1 m each with areas of cross section 1 mm2 are used. The wires are connected in series and one end of the combined wire is connected to a rigid support and other end is subjected to elongation. The stress required to produce a net elongation of 0.2 mm is,
[Given, the Young's Modulus for steel and brass are, respectively, 120×109 N/m2 and 60×109 N/m2 ]
solution:
Stress will be same in both rods
Stress =Y1Δl1l=Y2Δl2l … (1)
And
Δl1+Δl2=0.2×10-3 m … (2)
From equation (1) and (2)
Stress ×lY1+lY2=0.2×10-3 m
Stress ×1120×109+160×109=0.2×10-3 m
Stress =8×106 N/m2

subject: Physics
q: A wire suspended vertically from one of its ends is stretched by attaching a weight of $200 \mathrm{~N}$ to the lower end. The weight stretches the wire by $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the elastic energy stored in the wire is
question: A wire suspended vertically from one of its ends is stretched by attaching a weight of $200 \mathrm{~N}$ to the lower end. The weight stretches the wire by $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the elastic energy stored in the wire is
solution:
Elastic energy $=\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{x}$
$$
\mathrm{F}=200 \mathrm{~N}, \mathrm{x}=1 \mathrm{~mm}=10^{-3} \mathrm{~m} \quad \therefore \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}=0.1 \mathrm{~J}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Choose the correct relationship between Poisson ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>σ</mi></mfenced></math>, bulk modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>K</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and modulus of rigidity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>η</mi></mfenced></math> of a given solid object:
question: Choose the correct relationship between Poisson ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>σ</mi></mfenced></math>, bulk modulus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>K</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and modulus of rigidity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>η</mi></mfenced></math> of a given solid object:
solution:
The relations of Young's modulus, Y, bulk modulus, K, the modulus of rigidity, η and Poisson's ratio, σ are given as

Y=3K(1-2σ) …1 and 

Y=2η1+σ …2

From equation 1 and 2, we have

⇒2η1+σ=3K1-2σ

⇒σ=3K-2η6K+2η

subject: Physics
q: Steel ruptures when a shear of $3.5 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ is applied. The force needed to punch a $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ diameter hole in a steel sheet $0.3 \mathrm{~cm}$ thick is nearly:
question: Steel ruptures when a shear of $3.5 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ is applied. The force needed to punch a $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ diameter hole in a steel sheet $0.3 \mathrm{~cm}$ thick is nearly:
solution: Shearing strain is created along the side surface of the punched disk. Note that the forces exerted on the disk are exerted along the circumference of the disk, and the total force exerted on its center only. Let us assume that the shearing stress along the side surface of the disk is uniform, then $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{F}>& \int_{\text {surface }} \mathrm{dF}_{\max }=\int_{\text {surface }} \sigma_{\max } \mathrm{dA}=\sigma_{\max } \int_{\text {surface }} \mathrm{dA} \\ &=\int \sigma_{\max } \cdot \mathrm{A}=\sigma_{\max } \cdot 2 \pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{D}}{2}\right) \mathrm{h} \\ &=3.5 \times 10^8 \times\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-2}\right) \times 0.3 \times 10^{-2} \times 2 \pi \\ &=3.297 \times 10^4 \simeq 3.3 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q: A stone of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is projected from a rubber catapult of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and area of cross section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> stretched by an amount <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The velocity of the projected stone is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> (Young's modulus of rubber <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A stone of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is projected from a rubber catapult of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and area of cross section <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> stretched by an amount <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The velocity of the projected stone is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> (Young's modulus of rubber <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
By energy conservation
12·YAL·x2=12mv2
0.5×109×10-6×(0.04)20.1=201000v2
∴ v2=400
v=20 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: Two blocks of mass 2 kg and 4 kg are connected by a metal wire going over a smooth pulley as shown in figure. The radius of wire is 4.0×10-5 m and Young's modulus of the metal is 2.0×1011 N m-2. The longitudinal strain developed in the wire is 1απ. The value of α is _____. [Use g=10 m s-2)
question: Two blocks of mass 2 kg and 4 kg are connected by a metal wire going over a smooth pulley as shown in figure. The radius of wire is 4.0×10-5 m and Young's modulus of the metal is 2.0×1011 N m-2. The longitudinal strain developed in the wire is 1απ. The value of α is _____. [Use g=10 m s-2)
solution:
For the given system, T=2m1m2m1+m2g=803 N

Area of the wire, A=πr2=16π×10−10 m2

Strain =Δll=FAY=TAY

=80316π×10−10×2×1011=112π

⇒α=12

subject: Physics
q: The Young's modulus of a steel wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>11</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> . The wire is suspended from its support on a given planet. A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached to the free end of the wire. The acceleration due to gravity on the planet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> of its value on the earth. The elongation of wire is (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> on the earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> ):
question: The Young's modulus of a steel wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>11</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> . The wire is suspended from its support on a given planet. A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached to the free end of the wire. The acceleration due to gravity on the planet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> of its value on the earth. The elongation of wire is (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math> on the earth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> ):
solution:
The external force or tension F acting on the wire is its own weight. It is given by

 F= mg=4 kg×104 m s-2=10 N

The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material of the wire is given by

Y= FA∆LL= FL∆LA..........................(1)

where, A is the cross-sectional area, L is the original length and ∆L is the elongation of the wire.

Substitute the given values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the required elongation of the wire.

2×1011 N/m2= 10 N×6 m∆L×3 mm2×1 m2106 mm2∆L= 10 N×6 m2×1011 N/m2×3 mm2×1 m2106 mm2= 0.0001 m×103 mm1 m= 0.1 mm

q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long wire having cross-sectional area  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and Young's modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> is subjected to a load of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, then the elongation in the wire will be :
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long wire having cross-sectional area  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and Young's modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> is subjected to a load of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, then the elongation in the wire will be :
solution:
As we know, elongation in wire is given by,

∆l=FlAY

⇒∆l=250×1006.25×10-4×1010

⇒∆l=4×10-3 m

q: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is clamped at one end. If its other end is pulled by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, its length increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>. If the radius of the wire and the applied force both are reduced to half of their original values keeping original length constant, the increase in length will become:
question: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is clamped at one end. If its other end is pulled by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>, its length increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>. If the radius of the wire and the applied force both are reduced to half of their original values keeping original length constant, the increase in length will become:
solution:
The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material of the wire, in the first case, can be written as

Y=Fπr2lL   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

F=Yπr2×lL   ...2

For the second case, the Young's modulus is given by

Y=F/2πr2/4l'L   ...3

From equation (3), it follows that

F=Yl'L×2×πr24   ...4

From equations (2) and (4), it implies that

Yπr2×lL=Yl'L×2×πr24⇒l'=2l

subject: Physics
q:
The volume contraction of a solid copper cube of edge length 10 cm , when subjected to a hydraulic pressure of $7 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~Pa}$, would be ____ $\mathrm{mm}^3$.
(Given bulk modulus of copper $=1.4 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
question:
The volume contraction of a solid copper cube of edge length 10 cm , when subjected to a hydraulic pressure of $7 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~Pa}$, would be ____ $\mathrm{mm}^3$.
(Given bulk modulus of copper $=1.4 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{V}=\frac{7 \times 10^6}{1.4 \times 10^{11}} \times\left(10 \times 10^{-2}\right)^3 \\ & \Delta \mathrm{~V}=50 \mathrm{~mm}^3\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion (A) : Steel is used in the construction of buildings and bridges.
Reason (R) : Steel is more elastic and its elastic limit is high. 

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion (A) : Steel is used in the construction of buildings and bridges.
Reason (R) : Steel is more elastic and its elastic limit is high. 

In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is not correct
solution:
The relationship between Young's modulus, stress, and strain is:

stressstrain=Y

Since the elastic modulus of steel is more, for the same amount of stress, the strain will be less resulting in less deformation of buildings and bridges. Therefore, steel is used for construction of large structures. 

Hence, both assertion and reason are correct and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

subject: Physics
q: The strain-stress plot for materials $A, B, C$ and $D$ is shown in the figure. Which material has the largest Young's modulus ?
question: The strain-stress plot for materials $A, B, C$ and $D$ is shown in the figure. Which material has the largest Young's modulus ?
solution:
Young's modulus ($Y$) is defined as the ratio of stress to strain: $Y = \frac{\text{stress}}{\text{strain}}$.
In the given graph, the y-axis represents strain and the x-axis represents stress.
The slope of the line in a strain-stress graph is given by $m = \frac{\text{strain}}{\text{stress}}$.
Therefore, $Y = \frac{1}{\text{slope}}$.
To have the largest Young's modulus, the material must have the smallest slope in the strain-stress plot.
From the figure, line $C$ has the smallest angle with the stress axis (x-axis), meaning it has the minimum slope.
Since $Y \propto \frac{1}{\text{slope}}$, material $C$ has the largest Young's modulus.

q: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is hanging from a fixed support. The length changes to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> when masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> are suspended respectively from its free end. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is equal to
question: A wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is hanging from a fixed support. The length changes to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> when masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> are suspended respectively from its free end. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
From Hooke's law, ∆L=FLAY⇒∆L∝F. Therefore,

L2-LL1-L=m2gm1g=21  ⇒L=2L1-L2

subject: Physics
q:
The depth below the surface of sea to which a rubber ball be taken so as to decrease its volume by 0.02% is _____ m.

(Take density of sea water =103 kg m-3, Bulk modulus of rubber =9×108 N m-2, and g=10 m s-2)
question:
The depth below the surface of sea to which a rubber ball be taken so as to decrease its volume by 0.02% is _____ m.

(Take density of sea water =103 kg m-3, Bulk modulus of rubber =9×108 N m-2, and g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Bulk modulus is given by, B=-∆P∆VV

⇒∆P=-B∆VV

⇒ρgh=-B∆VV

⇒103×10×h=-9×108×-0.02100

⇒h=18 m

subject: Physics
q: A uniform metallic wire is elongated by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> when subjected to a linear force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The elongation, if its length and diameter is doubled and subjected to the same force will be _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A uniform metallic wire is elongated by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> when subjected to a linear force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The elongation, if its length and diameter is doubled and subjected to the same force will be _________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
F=Y.A·Δll⇒Δl=FY.A.·l⇒Δl=F.lY.πr2⇒Δl∝lr2

Δl2Δl1=l2l1r1r22=2122⇒Δl2Δl1=12⇒Δl2=Δl12

=0.042=0.02 m⇒Δl2=2 cm.

subject: Physics
q:
When a rubber ball is taken to a depth of _______ m in deep sea, its volume decreases by 0.5%
(The bulk modulus of rubber =9.8×108 N m-2 Density of sea water =103 kg m-3,g=9.8 m s-2)
question:
When a rubber ball is taken to a depth of _______ m in deep sea, its volume decreases by 0.5%
(The bulk modulus of rubber =9.8×108 N m-2 Density of sea water =103 kg m-3,g=9.8 m s-2)
solution:
-dVV=0.5100=0.005
B=-dPdVV=ρgh-dVV
9.8×108=103×9.8×h5×10-3⇒h=500 m

subject: Physics
q: A sample of a liquid is kept at 1 atm. It is compressed to 5 atm which leads to change of volume of $0.8 \mathrm{~cm}^3$. If the bulk modulus of the liquid is 2 GPa , the initial volume of the liquid was _______ litre. $\left(\right.$ Take $\left.1 \mathrm{~atm}=10^5 \mathrm{~Pa}\right)$
question: A sample of a liquid is kept at 1 atm. It is compressed to 5 atm which leads to change of volume of $0.8 \mathrm{~cm}^3$. If the bulk modulus of the liquid is 2 GPa , the initial volume of the liquid was _______ litre. $\left(\right.$ Take $\left.1 \mathrm{~atm}=10^5 \mathrm{~Pa}\right)$
solution:
Given, Initial pressure of liquid $\left(P_i\right)=1 \mathrm{~atm}$
Final pressure of liquid $\left(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}\right)=5 \mathrm{~atm}$
Change in pressure $(\mathrm{dP})=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}=4 \mathrm{~atm}$
$=4 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~Pa}$
Change in volume $(\mathrm{dV})=-0.8 \mathrm{~cm}^3$
Bulk modulus $(B)=2 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~Pa}$
Now, $B=\frac{-d P}{(d V / V)} \Rightarrow V=-B\left(\frac{d V}{d P}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow & V=-2 \times 10^9 \times \frac{\left(-0.8 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{4 \times 10^5} \\ & =4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^3=4 \text { litre }
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Two blocks of masses 3 kg and 5 kg are connected by a metal wire going over a smooth pulley. The breaking stress of the metal is 24π×102 N m-2. What is the minimum radius of the wire ?
( take g=10 m s-2
question:
Two blocks of masses 3 kg and 5 kg are connected by a metal wire going over a smooth pulley. The breaking stress of the metal is 24π×102 N m-2. What is the minimum radius of the wire ?
( take g=10 m s-2
solution:
Tension in the string =2m1m2m1+m2g

=2×3×5×108=752

Stress =TA

24π×102=752πr2

r2=7548×10-2

r2=0.015625

r=0.125 m=12.5 cm

subject: Physics
q: The elastic potential energy stored in a steel wire of length 20 m stretched through 2 cm is 80 J. The cross sectional area of the wire is _____ mm2. (Given, Y=2.0×1011 N m–2)
question: The elastic potential energy stored in a steel wire of length 20 m stretched through 2 cm is 80 J. The cross sectional area of the wire is _____ mm2. (Given, Y=2.0×1011 N m–2)
solution:
The formula to calculate the Young's modulus of the material is given by

Y=stressstrain=stress∆lL   ...1

The formula to calculate the potential energy U stored into the wire is given by

U=12×stress×strain×volume=12×stress×∆lL×A×L= 12×stress×A×∆l   ...2

From equation (1) and equation (2), it can be written that

U=12×Y×∆l2L×A   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) and solve to calculate the required cross-sectional area of the wire.

80 J=12×2.0×1011 Nm-2×0.02 m220 m×A⇒A= 2×80 J×202.0×1011×4×10-4 Nm-1=4×10-5 m2×106 mm21 m2= 40 mm2

subject: Physics
q: A metal wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> has breaking stress <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. A block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached at one end of the string and is rotating in a horizontal circle. The maximum linear velocity of block will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A metal wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> has breaking stress <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. A block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached at one end of the string and is rotating in a horizontal circle. The maximum linear velocity of block will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Here in horizontal circular motion, the only centripetal force is tension. So,T=mω2r=mv2l=10×v20.5=20v2

Now, Tmax= Breaking stress × Area

⇒20v2=5×108×10-4

⇒20v2=5×104

Thus, the maximum linear velocity of block is 

v=14104=50 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: Two slabs with square cross section of different materials $(1,2)$ with equal sides $(l)$ and thickness $\mathrm{d}_1$ and $\mathrm{d}_2$ such that $\mathrm{d}_2=2 \mathrm{~d}_1$ and $l \gt \mathrm{d}_2$. Considering lower edges of these slabs are fixed to the floor, we apply equal shearing force on the narrow faces. The angle of deformation is $\theta_2=2 \theta_1$. If the shear moduli of material 1 is $4 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$, then shear moduli of material 2 is $\mathrm{x} \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$, where value of $x$ is ________.
question: Two slabs with square cross section of different materials $(1,2)$ with equal sides $(l)$ and thickness $\mathrm{d}_1$ and $\mathrm{d}_2$ such that $\mathrm{d}_2=2 \mathrm{~d}_1$ and $l \gt \mathrm{d}_2$. Considering lower edges of these slabs are fixed to the floor, we apply equal shearing force on the narrow faces. The angle of deformation is $\theta_2=2 \theta_1$. If the shear moduli of material 1 is $4 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$, then shear moduli of material 2 is $\mathrm{x} \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$, where value of $x$ is ________.
solution:
Deformation angle
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 \theta_1=\theta_2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \frac{\sigma_1}{\eta_1}=\frac{\sigma_2}{\eta_2}
\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 2\left(\frac{\mathrm{~F}}{\ell \mathrm{~d}_1 \eta_1}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\ell \mathrm{d}_2 \eta_2} \\ & \Rightarrow \eta_2=\frac{\eta_1}{4}=1 \times 10^9 \Rightarrow x=1\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The length of a metal wire is ℓ1, when the tension in it is T1 and is ℓ2 when the tension is T2. The natural length of the wire is:
question: The length of a metal wire is ℓ1, when the tension in it is T1 and is ℓ2 when the tension is T2. The natural length of the wire is:
solution:
T1=kℓ1-ℓ0

T2=kℓ2-ℓ0

T1 T2=ℓ1-ℓ0ℓ2-ℓ0

T1ℓ2-T1ℓ0= T2ℓ1-T2ℓ0

T2ℓ1-T1ℓ2 T2-T2=ℓ0

subject: Physics
q: A square aluminium (shear modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>) slab of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is subjected to a shearing force (on its narrow face) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The lower edge is riveted to the floor. The displacement of the upper edge is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: A square aluminium (shear modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>) slab of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is subjected to a shearing force (on its narrow face) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The lower edge is riveted to the floor. The displacement of the upper edge is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
Shearing stress is given by FA=ηxl

Thus, displacement is x=FlAη

⇒x=18×104×60×10-260×10-2×15×10-2×25×109

=48×10-6 m=48 μm

subject: Physics
q: A boy’s catapult is made of rubber cord which is <math><mn>42</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long, with <math><mn>6</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> diameter of cross-section and of negligible mass. The boy keeps a stone weighing <math><mn>0.02</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> on it and stretches the cord by <math><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> by applying a constant force. When released, the stone flies off with a velocity of <math><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . Neglect the change in the area of cross-section of the cord while stretched. The Young’s modulus of rubber is closest to:
question: A boy’s catapult is made of rubber cord which is <math><mn>42</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long, with <math><mn>6</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> diameter of cross-section and of negligible mass. The boy keeps a stone weighing <math><mn>0.02</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> on it and stretches the cord by <math><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> by applying a constant force. When released, the stone flies off with a velocity of <math><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . Neglect the change in the area of cross-section of the cord while stretched. The Young’s modulus of rubber is closest to:
solution:
Elastic potential energy =12.Y. ∆lL2AL
=12Y. 0.20.422.π×32×10-6×0.42
=1.35×10-6Y
Kinetic energy =12mv2=12×(0.02×202)
1.35×10-6 Y=4
Y=3×106 N m-2

subject: Physics
q: A wire of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> is stretched between two clamps <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> apart. The resulting strain in the wire is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The lowest frequency of the transverse vibrations in the wire (Young's modulus of wire  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ), (to the nearest integer),_______
question: A wire of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> is stretched between two clamps <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> apart. The resulting strain in the wire is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The lowest frequency of the transverse vibrations in the wire (Young's modulus of wire  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ), (to the nearest integer),_______
solution:
Fundamental frequency in the string

f=12ℓTμ=12ℓTρA=12ℓYΔℓρℓ
f=12ℓYΔℓρℓ
=129×1010×4.9×10-49×10-3×10-6=35

subject: Physics
q: Young's moduli of two wires <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are in the ratio <math><mi> </mi><mn>7</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>4</mn></math> . Wire <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long and has radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Wire <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long and has radius <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the two wires stretch by the same length for a given load, the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is close to:
question: Young's moduli of two wires <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are in the ratio <math><mi> </mi><mn>7</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>4</mn></math> . Wire <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long and has radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Wire <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long and has radius <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the two wires stretch by the same length for a given load, the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is close to:
solution:
Strain = ∆l/l
Stress =F/A
Young’s modulus =StressStrain=F/A∆l/l=FAl∆l
YAYB=lAAAABlB=74
2πrA2πrB21.5=74
rA2=21.5×7×2 mm2
rA=1.7 mm
